hook: You already know how to work with sets and functions — unions, intersections, injections, surjections — but so far you've mostly used them as tools. This unit asks a deeper question: how do you *prove* that these objects behave the way you think they do? By the end, you'll encounter one of the most astonishing results in all of mathematics: that some infinities are genuinely larger than others.
concept: The unifying idea is using rigorous proof techniques to establish truths about the fundamental building blocks of mathematics — sets, functions, and size — captured formally by the notion of *structured logical argument over abstract objects*.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to rigorously verify equalities like $A \cap (B \cup C) = (A \cap B) \cup (A \cap C)$ by writing element-chasing proofs and double-containment arguments.
Master the technique of formally proving that a given function is injective, surjective, or bijective directly from the definitions, rather than relying on intuition.
Apply bijection-based arguments to compare the sizes of sets, proving surprising results such as the fact that $\mathbb{N}$ and $\mathbb{Z}$ have the same cardinality.
Understand and reconstruct Cantor's elegant diagonal proof that the real numbers are uncountable — establishing that $|\mathbb{R}| > |\mathbb{N}|$ and that not all infinities are equal.
